claim: The spec is unimplementable as written without turning a FROZEN suite red: F5's frozen scope guard TEST-070 (tests/shared/registry-no-renderer-ui.test.ts) greps EVERY file under src/renderer/ for OrkyRegistrySnapshot|OrkyRegistryEntry|onRegistryStatus|registryCurrent(|registryAddRoot( and asserts zero matches 'and MUST keep passing after this feature ships'. REQ-003 mandates exactly those symbols in src/renderer/ (App.tsx onRegistryStatus subscription + registryCurrent() recovery pull) and the Public interface adds registrySnapshot: OrkyRegistrySnapshot to the renderer store. The spec's Baseline-fit section claims the feature is purely additive and plans no retirement/supersession of this guard, so implement is guaranteed to hit a frozen-test collision with no sanctioned resolution path — the exact frozen-suite-collision class the spec's own REQ-017 polices for NEW freeze tests but ignores for this existing one.
fix: Add a normative note (Baseline fit or a REQ) that F6 supersedes and retires TEST-070 atomically in the same change (per CONV-012's co-ownership principle), recording the arbitration: the F5 REQ-021 guard was scoped to F5's development window and F6 is its designed first consumer (the test's own header says 'F6 is the first UI consumer'). propose CONV: a frozen scope-guard test asserting the ABSENCE of a future consumer ('no X consumes Y yet') MUST name the feature expected to retire it, and that consumer feature's spec MUST retire/supersede the guard atomically in the same change.
resolution: REQ-019 (new) normatively supersedes TEST-070 at F6's tests phase, by the test-designer, in the same change as F6's suite, with the arbitration recorded in Baseline fit and the proposed CONV captured for doc-sync promotion.

claim: REQ-009 mandates the pane's project path come from baseline resolveProjectKey order (git root FIRST, then live/persisted cwd), but the aggregate's own pane->root binding is findOrkyRoot(cwd) — an upward .orky/ walk from the pane's cwd (src/main/orky/find-orky-root.ts). Whenever an .orky project root lies BENEATH the pane's git root (a monorepo/brownfield-adopted subproject), resolveProjectKey returns the outer git root, which is not equal-or-beneath the aggregate root, so matchPaneRoot returns null: click-to-focus silently fails and REQ-010's fallback spawns duplicate terminals for a project that HAS an open, main-side-bound (source:'pane') pane. REQ-009's own acceptance ('a pane under nested root R2 c R1, both members, matches R2 only') contradicts the git-root-first rule in the same layout — if the pane's git root is R1 the mandated project path IS R1, so it matches R1, not R2; the criterion is only satisfiable by fixturing gitStatus absent, i.e. the acceptance tests a different rule than the normative text specifies.
fix: Redefine the match to try the pane's candidate paths against the root set in a containment-correct order — e.g. match the live/persisted cwd (the same signal findOrkyRoot binds on) and fall back to / union with the git root — so renderer matching agrees with the aggregate's own source:'pane' semantics. Add an acceptance vector where the git root is a strict ANCESTOR of the aggregate root (pane cwd inside the project, gitStatus.root above it) asserting the pane still matches.
location: 02-spec.md:REQ-009 (normative text vs acceptance; also REQ-010 acceptance 'matched project shows none')
resolution: REQ-009 now mandates cwd-first candidate order (live cwd, persisted config.cwd, git root last) with longest-root containment matching — the aggregate's own findOrkyRoot signal — and its acceptance adds the strict-ancestor-git-root vector; REQ-010 defines "matched" by the same matcher; Baseline fit records the scoped divergence from baseline REQ-022.

claim: matchPaneRoot's pinned signature (panePath, roots) => string|null cannot implement the REQ's platform-CONDITIONAL fold: normalizeProjectRoot folds case only when process.platform === 'win32', but the new module is required to be pure (no DOM, no Electron) and Termhalla's contextIsolated renderer main world has NO process global (verified: zero process.platform/navigator reads anywhere in src/renderer or src/shared today, and preload exposes no platform signal). The natural implementation copies the process.platform conditional — which is defined in vitest/jsdom (all of the spec's unit/component tests pass) but is a ReferenceError or misfold in the real renderer bundle, and the e2e list (toggle/mosaic/badge/recovery/byte-identical) never exercises click-to-focus, so a runtime-broken matcher ships green. The 'matcher-vs-normalizeProjectRoot equivalence pinned by test vectors' criterion is additionally host-platform-dependent (path.resolve treats backslashes as literal name chars on posix CI, silently weakening the vectors), and the enumerated vectors omit trailing separators, UNC (\server\share) roots, and drive-root edge cases.
fix: Change the pinned signature to inject the fold mode explicitly (e.g. matchPaneRoot(panePath, roots, opts: {caseFold: boolean}) or platform: 'win32'|'posix'), pin HOW the renderer caller obtains it (a preload-exposed constant or an existing signal — pick one and verify it exists), and require the equivalence harness to use path.win32/path.posix explicitly so vectors are host-independent; add explicit vectors for trailing separators, UNC roots, and a drive-letter root, covering BOTH fold modes.
resolution: The pinned matchPaneRoot signature now injects opts.caseFold, the renderer derivation is pinned to caseFoldFromPlatform(navigator.platform) (verified against source: zero process/navigator reads under src/renderer/, preload exposes no platform constant, navigator exists in both runtimes), REQ-009 mandates host-independent path.win32/path.posix equivalence vectors covering trailing separators, a UNC root, a drive-letter root and both fold modes, and the e2e list now exercises click-to-focus in the packaged renderer where process is undefined.

claim: The spec never pins which snapshot wins when the ONE registryCurrent() recovery pull settles AFTER a newer registry:status push has already been applied. REQ-008's deep-equal short-circuit only guards the EQUAL case: a pull result computed before a subsequent recompute is NOT deep-equal to the newer pushed snapshot, so an unconditional .then(setRegistrySnapshot) regresses the store to stale data (wrong badge/list) until the next push. Correctness currently rests on an unstated Electron invoke-reply-vs-send ordering assumption; determinism is a declared lens of this spec and no acceptance criterion exercises a late-settling pull.
fix: Add a normative sentence to REQ-003/REQ-011: the recovery pull's result MUST be discarded if any snapshot (push or pull) has already been applied (first-valid-snapshot-wins; pushes thereafter are the sole source), plus an acceptance where the pull resolves with a stale snapshot after a push was applied and the store/DOM provably keeps the pushed data.
resolution: REQ-003 now specifies a monotonic-generation guard (pull records issue-time generation; result discarded if any snapshot applied since; first valid snapshot wins; no invoke-vs-send ordering assumption load-bearing) with a stale-late-settling-pull acceptance vector, echoed at the DOM level in REQ-011.

claim: CONV-011 violation as specified: paneFocusSeq: Record<paneId, number> is a per-key map mirroring the live open-pane membership set ('updated on every setFocusedPane'), but the spec requires no pruning when a pane closes — over a long-lived session (the norm for a terminal app) it grows unbounded, and a reused pane id would inherit a stale recency rank. Only open panes are match candidates today, so the impact is a session-lifetime leak rather than a wrong focus, but CONV-011 makes pruning a standing requirement, not a preference.
fix: Add to REQ-009: entries in the focus-sequence map MUST be pruned when their pane leaves the store (pane close/workspace removal), with an acceptance assertion (close a focused pane, assert its key is gone).
resolution: REQ-009 now mandates paneFocusSeq entries be pruned when their pane leaves the store (pane close / workspace removal, CONV-011), with an acceptance asserting the closed pane's key is gone; the Public interface pins the same.

claim: The FINDING-001 repair re-creates the same trap one feature downstream: REQ-019's mandated NARROWED replacement guard freezes an absence-assertion over the registry mutation surface (RegistryMutationResult / registryRoots( / registryAddRoot( / registryRemoveRoot() for ALL of src/renderer/, while the spec's own Open question #2 records that the human 'track this project' gesture — a renderer-side registry:addRoot/registryRoots consumer, plausibly F13 Settings — is coming but unassigned. REQ-019 requires the replacement guard's header to carry the TEST-070 supersession note but NOT to name the feature expected to retire the NEW guard, violating the spec's own proposed convention from FINDING-001 ('a frozen scope-guard test asserting the ABSENCE of a future consumer MUST name the feature expected to retire it'), so the track-project feature will hit an identical frozen-suite collision with no named resolution path.
fix: Amend REQ-019: the replacement guard's header MUST also name its own designated retirement path — e.g. 'superseded in turn by the feature that ships the track-project gesture (currently unassigned; see 02-spec.md Open question #2, plausibly F13 Settings), via the same tests-phase supersession protocol' — so the future consumer inherits a sanctioned supersession route instead of a bare frozen collision. Alternatively scope the guard's header to state it guards REQ-017's F6-window prohibition and is retirable by any feature whose spec normatively supersedes it.
resolution: REQ-019 amended (delegated-human edit, pre-gate): the narrowed replacement guard MUST name its own designated retiring feature in its header, so an absence-of-consumer guard is never frozen without a scheduled retirement path — per the convention this feature itself proposes.

claim: buildDecisionQueue admits any record with needsHuman===true and a non-empty feature string, then casts it to OrkyFeatureStatus — features with missing/mistyped CONSUMED fields (reason, phase, gateN/gateM, lastActivityAt, detail) still become queue items, contradicting REQ-013 (and the function's own header comment) that a mistyped feature contributes no items; garbage fields then flow into compareOrkyFeatures group ranking (potential NaN/undefined comparisons — a REQ-006 determinism edge) and into the panel rows F8 will attach actions to
fix: Validate the consumed field set before admission (reason string|null, phase string|null, gateN/gateM numbers, lastActivityAt number, detail string|undefined — per the spec's Verified contract), or coerce mistyped optional fields to safe defaults at the boundary; keep the needsHuman===true membership rule
verified_by: codex-cross-check (filed by coordinator after independent verification; codex hit a findings-lock EPERM)
resolution: Merged into FINDING-021 — same defect (unvalidated consumed fields on admitted features); FINDING-021 carries the contract_violation flag a MUST violation requires and the fuller consequence analysis (React child crash + NaN comparator).

claim: REQ-014 MUST violation: the open-terminal-here fallback affordance is NOT activatable by Enter/Space. The fallback <button> is nested inside the item row (role=button) whose onKeyDown handler fires on BUBBLED keydowns with no e.target guard: Enter/Space on the focused fallback button hits the row handler, whose e.preventDefault() cancels the native button activation (the keydown default action), then calls focusProject() — which by construction no-ops, since the fallback only renders when NO pane matches. Net: keyboard activation is a silent no-op, also violating REQ-010 (never a silent no-op). Compounding: ARIA role=button is a Children-Presentational role, so the nested native button is stripped from the accessibility tree — screen-reader users cannot perceive the fallback as a distinct control at all. This shipped green because REQ-014/REQ-010 tests are source-scan greps (TEST-348/TEST-351) and the e2e (TEST-367) activates the fallback via .click() only; no test presses Enter/Space on it.
fix: Guard the row keydown (if (e.target !== e.currentTarget) return) AND restructure so the fallback is not an interactive descendant of a role=button element (e.g. make the row a plain container whose text region is the activation button, with the fallback button as a sibling). Add a behavioral keyboard test: focus decision-queue-open-terminal, press Enter, assert a terminal pane spawns at the project root.
resolution: Verified fixed (ux re-verification): the fallback is a native <button> SIBLING inside a non-interactive pane-less row — no role="button" anywhere in the panel (no Children-Presentational stripping) and no ancestor keydown to preventDefault its native Enter/Space activation. The matched row keydown target-guards (e.target !== e.currentTarget early-return, DecisionQueuePanel.tsx:178) and matched rows have no focusable descendants, so row-level Enter/Space activation of focusProject is intact; Tab order runs close button -> items in document order. Pinned by TEST-371 (structural: guard literal, no role="button", native fallback button, row tabIndex) and TEST-372 (packaged e2e: Tab reaches the fallback, Enter spawns the terminal at the project root).

claim: The queue badge count is invisible to assistive technology: the toggle sets aria-label="Toggle Orky decision queue", and aria-label OVERRIDES element content in accessible-name computation, so the visible amber count (the feature's core needs-you signal, REQ-007) is never announced — a screen-reader user gets zero indication that anything is waiting, and no update when the count changes while the drawer is closed. Sighted-only information parity gap on the primary signaling surface.
fix: Fold the live count into the accessible name (e.g. aria-label={`Toggle Orky decision queue${queueCount > 0 ? `, ${queueCount} waiting` : ""}`}) or give the badge an aria-live polite status region; assert the accessible name reflects the count in a test.
resolution: Verified fixed (ux re-verification): StatusBar.tsx:103 folds the live count into the toggle’s accessible name (aria-label "Toggle Orky decision queue, N waiting" when N>0), recomputed via the subscribed memoized queueCount selector on every count change — the finding’s first proposed fix. Name recomputation is the right mechanism here: an aria-live badge would interject announcements while the user types in a terminal, and polite-live inside a button is unreliable across AT. Residual (noted, below MEDIUM on its own): no test asserts the aria-label carries the count — TEST-365 pins only the visible badge text and aria-expanded.

claim: No focus management on open or close: toggling the drawer via mod+shift+o or the palette leaves focus wherever it was — in the common case inside an xterm terminal, which CONSUMES Tab — so the just-opened drawer is keyboard-unreachable in real use; REQ-014's "keyboard-only navigation reaches and activates an item" only holds when focus already sits in chrome (e.g. the status bar). Closing via the drawer's close button unmounts the focused element, dropping focus to <body> instead of returning it to orky-queue-toggle. Mirrors the notes drawer's existing behavior, but F6 is the feature whose spec promises an end-to-end keyboard path, and no jsdom/e2e test exercises Tab-reachability from a focused terminal (the structural suite is source-scan only), so the gap ships green.
fix: On keyboard-initiated open (chord or palette), move focus into the drawer (the close button or first queue item); when the close button closes the drawer, restore focus to the orky-queue-toggle. propose CONV: a chrome drawer/panel opened via a keyboard command (chord or palette entry) MUST move focus into the opened surface and restore focus to its toggle on close, so the gesture is keyboard-operable end-to-end.
resolution: Verified fixed (ux re-verification): the panel mounts only while open (App.tsx:168), so its mount effect (DecisionQueuePanel.tsx:31-40) focuses the drawer close button on EVERY open path — chord (App.tsx:133), palette (CommandPalette.tsx:64, which does no focus restore of its own on close), and status-bar toggle click — and the unmount cleanup restores focus on every close path (close button, chord, palette, toggle; no Escape-close path exists for this drawer, matching the notes drawer). An unmounted previously-focused element is handled by the document.contains(prev) guard with fallback to orky-queue-toggle. Residuals: the focus management is pinned by no test (TEST-372 explicitly re-focuses the toggle rather than relying on focus-in), and the UNCONDITIONAL restore introduces a non-modal focus-yank defect on the drawer’s own click-to-focus flow — filed separately as FINDING-023.

claim: Rows for pane-less projects present as fully interactive (role=button, tabIndex=0, cursor:pointer, hover highlight) yet activating them does nothing: focusProject() finds no matching pane and returns. Because role=button makes descendants presentational, this inert row is the ONLY control AT exposes for a pane-less project. REQ-009's "with no matching pane the click performs no focus change (the fallback affordance path applies instead)" is ambiguous between "row activation delegates to the fallback" and "row activation is inert"; the implementation chose inert, leaving a dead-feeling affordance for exactly the projects the drawer most needs to surface (source: persisted, no pane).
fix: Delegate row activation to openTerminalAt(projectRoot) when no pane matches (the natural reading of "the fallback path applies instead"), or strip role/tabIndex/cursor/hover from inert rows; clarify the REQ-009 sentence either way.
resolution: Verified fixed (ux re-verification): the pane-less row is now a plain container — no tabIndex, no role, no cursor:pointer, no .dq-row class (so neither the hover highlight at index.css:220 nor the focus ring at index.css:216 applies) and no click/key handlers; the native "open terminal here" button is the sole, honest activation surface (the finding’s strip-the-affordance alternative). Matched rows keep pointer/hover/focus-ring, which is honest — they ARE interactive — and the visible per-row button keeps the pane-less/matched distinction legible.

claim: While the drawer is open, the entire panel re-renders and re-runs the full pane-to-root matching on every mousedown in any pane and on every per-pane runtime churn: (a) the panel subscribes to paneFocusSeq via a useStore hook even though that map is read ONLY inside the focusProject click handler (it never affects rendered output), and setFocusedPane - wired to PaneTile's onMouseDownCapture - unconditionally builds a new paneFocusSeq object and bumps the counter even when the clicked pane is already focused, so every single click inside any terminal invalidates that subscription and re-renders the whole drawer; (b) memberRoots and paneMatches are computed inline with no useMemo, so each such render re-folds every member root for every candidate of every pane (~panes x 3 x (roots+1) foldPath regex/split passes) plus a per-group paneMatches.some scan. The drawer-closed default is unaffected (panel unmounted; badge selector value-stable) and per-render cost is bounded (sub-millisecond at typical sizes), but this puts avoidable recompute + reconciliation work on the mousedown-to-focus interaction path and it scales with open panes x tracked projects x queue size. REQ-008's memoization mandate covers only the snapshot selectors (which ARE correctly memoized), so this is an implementation-level render-path regression, not a spec violation.
fix: Read paneFocusSeq (handler-only state) via useStore.getState() inside focusProject instead of subscribing with a hook; wrap the memberRoots/paneMatches derivation in useMemo keyed on [registrySnapshot, workspaces, order, cwds, gitStatus, caseFold]; optionally have setFocusedPane skip the stamp when paneId === s.focusedPaneId (the currently focused pane already holds the maximum sequence, so MRU semantics are unchanged). propose CONV: store state that a component reads only inside event handlers MUST be read via getState() at event time, never subscribed with a render hook.
resolution: Fixed: paneFocusSeq read via useStore.getState() in the click handler only; memberRoots/paneMatches useMemo-ized. Verified sound by the devils-advocate re-verification cross-check (DecisionQueuePanel.tsx:86).

claim: The deep-equal short-circuit is correctly placed BEFORE any snapshot state write and preserves the registrySnapshot reference plus the memoized queueGroups/queueCount (REQ-008 holds for every current subscriber), but the branch still performs a zustand state write per value-identical push - set(s => ({snapshotGeneration: s.snapshotGeneration + 1})) - replacing the root state object and re-running every mounted subscriber's selector. Value-identical pushes are the steady-state common case: main's OrkyRegistry.recompute() emits unconditionally on every debounced (300ms/root) re-read with no value dedup, so this runs for the whole session at push frequency. The generation's only consumer is the ONE mount-time recovery pull (App.tsx issues registryCurrent() once; applyRecoveryPull compares against the issue-time generation), so after that pull settles or is discarded the bump is permanent dead work on the hottest push path (~tens of microseconds per push today). It also makes any FUTURE subscriber of snapshotGeneration - or a bare useStore() - silently re-render on every value-identical push, eroding REQ-008's zero-re-render guarantee by one selector choice.
fix: Make the deep-equal branch a pure no-op once the recovery pull can no longer land: e.g. a slice-closure pullSettled flag set by applyRecoveryPull/recoveryPullFailed (or a pullOutstanding counter), gating the generation bump. REQ-003's arbitration is intact because the generation only needs to advance while a pull is still in flight; the guarded set also stops notifying every store subscriber per push in steady state.
resolution: Fixed: pullSettled flag gates the deep-equal generation bump after the recovery pull settles. Devils-advocate cross-check stress-tested StrictMode double-pull and interleavings — generation-guard semantics preserved (registry-slice.ts:60,83,97,104).

claim: The item's feature/reason/phase line and its detail line are both ellipsized (nowrap + overflow hidden) with NO title/tooltip or wrap affordance, in a fixed 340px drawer. The detail string is exactly the CONV-001 "specific + actionable" text (e.g. a full escalation question) — the datum the user opens the queue to read — and it is unrecoverable once clipped. The group header DOES carry title={projectRoot} (REQ-005), so the omission is inconsistent within the same component.
fix: Add title={it.status.detail} to the detail div (and a title on the feature line or row), or let the detail line wrap to a bounded number of lines.
resolution: Verified fixed (ux re-verification): the detail line carries title={it.status.detail} (DecisionQueuePanel.tsx:161), so the clipped CONV-001 detail — the datum the user opens the queue to read — is recoverable on hover. Residual (below LOW threshold): the feature/reason/phase line remains ellipsized without a title; mitigated by the group header title={projectRoot} and the slug’s near-front position in the 340px drawer.

claim: The frozen test's feat() fixture helper types its override param as Partial<OrkyFeatureStatus> & { feature: string } and then writes 'feature: over.feature,' as an explicit property before spreading '...over' last. Because 'feature' is non-optional on the intersection, tsc proves it will always be overwritten by the spread and raises TS2783 ('feature' is specified more than once, so this usage will be overwritten) at line 17 col 5 — verified directly by running tsc --noEmit against the file with the project's own compiler options. It is currently invisible to both project gates: package.json's typecheck script scopes tsc to tsconfig.json (src/renderer, src/shared, src/preload only, no tests/) and vitest performs no type-checking, so 'npm run typecheck' and 'npm test' both stay green. The sibling 0004 fixture (tests/shared/orky-status.test.ts's own feat()) avoids this by typing its override as a fully-Partial<{...}> object with no required field, so the spread can never be proven redundant — this feature's frozen fixture deviates from that established, TS2783-safe pattern. Functionally harmless (identical value on both sides of the redundant assignment), but it is a real diagnostic that would break the moment tests/ is ever added to a typecheck scope or a stricter lint gate.,
resolution: Malformed filing artifact (shell-quoting failure truncated the claim and dropped fix/phase_of_origin/verified_by). Superseded by FINDING-018, the complete re-filing of the same TS2783 frozen-fixture issue.

claim: buildDecisionQueue admits a feature after validating ONLY needsHuman === true and a non-empty feature string (decision-queue.ts:62), so the per-feature tolerance mandated by REQ-013 (a feature missing/mistyping the consumed fields contributes no items) is not implemented. Determinism consequence: for an admitted feature whose lastActivityAt is missing/mistyped, compareOrkyFeatures returns NaN (orky-status.ts:287, undefined minus number); an unknown non-null reason string does the same via the REASON_RANK lookup yielding undefined (orky-status.ts:285-286). The group sort comparator (decision-queue.ts:72-76) then returns NaN because the guard byRank !== 0 is TRUE for NaN, and Array.prototype.sort coerces a NaN comparator result to +0 (ES2023 CompareArrayElements), so the REQ-005-mandated root-codepoint tie-break is silently bypassed: cross-group order degrades to snapshot entry order, and with an inconsistent comparator ECMA-262 makes the whole sort order implementation-defined. The exact property TEST-309 pins (shuffle-invariance, one order from 100 shuffled rebuilds) fails for such fixtures, and the rendered row also carries undefined gate text. TEST-312 covers only a feature MISSING needsHuman, never one carrying needsHuman:true with mistyped comparator fields, so the gap ships green.
fix: Validate the comparator-consumed fields at admission — Number.isFinite(f.lastActivityAt) and reason being one of escalation/stalled/human-review/null — so a mistyped feature contributes no items per REQ-013; or NaN-harden the group rank (fall through to the root tie-break unless Number.isFinite(byRank)). Add a frozen vector: two groups whose top items carry a missing/mistyped lastActivityAt (and one with an unknown reason string) must still order by root codepoint across seeded shuffles.
resolution: Merged into FINDING-021 — same defect; see FINDING-007 merge note.

location: src/renderer/store.ts:170 (applyAssignment move-away path) vs REQ-009 pruning model
claim: paneFocusSeq is pruned only via clearPaneRuntime, which is reached from closePane (store.ts:430) and closeWorkspace (store.ts:259). The applyAssignment move-away path deletes every workspace this window no longer owns (store.ts:170) WITHOUT clearPaneRuntime, so focus-rank keys for the departed panes outlive the open-pane set — REQ-009 states the map never outlives the open-pane set (CONV-011) — and a workspace redocked into this window resumes pre-departure ranks instead of ranking as never-focused. Deterministic and bounded: only currently-open panes are MRU candidates (selectMruPane consults focusSeq solely for panes present in paneMatches), so no wrong pick occurs while the panes are away, and the identical leak pre-exists on this path for the other eight per-pane runtime maps (statuses/cwds/procs/aiSessions/usage/recording/gitStatus/exited). But it is the one pane-departure path the spec enumeration (pane close / workspace removal) missed, and TEST-341 pins only the closePane route.
fix: Route the applyAssignment dropped-workspace branch through clearPaneRuntime for the departing pane ids (also curing the pre-existing runtime-map leak on undock), or amend REQ-009 to enumerate window reassignment explicitly and add a redock acceptance vector (undock a focused workspace, redock it, assert its panes rank as never-focused).
resolution: Fixed: applyAssignment move-away branch routes departed panes through clearPaneRuntime (all nine runtime maps incl. paneFocusSeq), spread only when non-empty; PTYs correctly preserved. Verified by devils-advocate cross-check (store.ts:174-199).

claim: The frozen test's feat() fixture helper types its override param as Partial<OrkyFeatureStatus> & { feature: string } and writes 'feature: over.feature,' as an explicit property before spreading '...over' last. Because 'feature' is non-optional on the intersection, tsc proves it will always be overwritten by the spread and raises TS2783 ("'feature' is specified more than once, so this usage will be overwritten") at line 17 col 5 - verified directly by running tsc --noEmit against the file with the project's own compiler options (target/module/strict/paths). This is currently invisible to both project gates: package.json's typecheck script scopes tsc to tsconfig.json (src/renderer, src/shared, src/preload only - no tests/) and vitest performs no type-checking, so 'npm run typecheck' and 'npm test' both stay green with this diagnostic present. The sibling 0004 fixture (tests/shared/orky-status.test.ts's own feat()) avoids this by typing its override as a fully-Partial<{...}> object with no required field, so the spread can never be proven redundant - this feature's frozen fixture deviates from that established, TS2783-safe pattern. Functionally harmless (both sides of the redundant assignment hold the identical value), but it is a real diagnostic that would break the moment tests/ is ever added to a typecheck scope or a stricter lint/CI gate is introduced.
fix: At the next sanctioned tests-phase touch to this file (never an implementation-phase edit of a frozen test, per ADR-009), drop the redundant explicit 'feature: over.feature,' from feat()'s return object and rely solely on '...over' (whose type already guarantees 'feature' is present) - matching the TS2783-safe pattern already used by tests/shared/orky-status.test.ts's own feat() helper (type the override as Partial<{...}> with no required field, or otherwise avoid explicitly pre-assigning any field the override type requires).
resolution: Fixed by the test-designer in the sanctioned ESC-001 loopback touch: the feat() fixture no longer sets the redundant explicit feature: key (0004 pattern); npx tsc --noEmit over the touched test files exits 0.

claim: This feature's edit adds an unused optional 'label?: string' field to PaletteItem's 'dir' variant, justified by the inline comment "declared so the union exposes label uniformly for consumers that read it off any item" - but no consumer reads item.label generically. buildPaletteItems() (quick.ts:112,116) never sets 'label' on any dir item it constructs, and the ONLY renderer read site, CommandPalette.tsx's per-item label computation (lines 100-102), branches explicitly on item.kind and derives the dir row's display text from item.path, not item.label ('${item.favorite ? "*" : "clock"} ${item.path}'). The field is therefore dead: never written, never read, and not required by any REQ in 02-spec.md or TASK in 03-plan.md (TASK-011 only concerns the palette entry for toggle-orky-queue). Checked separately: this addition does not weaken any TS exhaustiveness check on PaletteAction/PaletteItem.kind - CommandPalette.tsx's activate() (lines 57-75) and the render-label ternary (lines 100-102) are both plain if/ternary chains with no 'never'-typed exhaustiveness assertion in this codebase (grep for assertNever/exhaustive across src/ and tests/ finds none related to these unions), so there was no exhaustiveness guard to weaken - but the field itself is still speculative, unused surface added to a shared, multi-owner type with a justification that does not match actual usage.
fix: Remove the unused 'label?: string' field from the 'dir' variant of PaletteItem in src/shared/quick.ts (revert to '{ kind: "dir"; path: string; favorite: boolean; search: string }') unless a concrete, currently-planned consumer needs it; if a future feature does need a dir-item label, add the field in that feature's own change together with the read site that uses it, so the type and its consumer land atomically (YAGNI - avoid speculative unused surface on a shared file).
resolution: Comment corrected to the truth: label? is not dead — it absorbs frozen TEST-329 reading item!.label off the un-narrowed PaletteItem union; removal breaks typecheck. Retirement path named in the comment (sanctioned tests-phase narrowing of TEST-329).

claim: REQ-009 MUST violation: the spec conditions candidate (2) persisted config.cwd on 'when no live cwd is known' and candidate (3) gitStatus.root on 'ONLY when neither cwd signal exists' — availability gating. The implementation supplies all three candidates unconditionally and matchPaneRootFromCandidates falls through on MATCH FAILURE, so a pane whose live cwd IS known but lies outside every member root is still bound via its stale persisted config.cwd or git root. That re-creates exactly the drift the FINDING-002 repair pinned cwd-first matching to prevent: a pane that cd'd out of the project after the aggregate bound it is unbound main-side (findOrkyRoot(live cwd) = null), yet the renderer still matches it — click-to-focus focuses a pane whose actual cwd is elsewhere, and the REQ-010 fallback affordance is suppressed (REQ-010 acceptance defines matched = 'a pane whose live cwd is under the root'). The tests phase introduced the contradiction: frozen TEST-324 line 138 pins the fall-through for the seam ('First candidate matches nothing -> the next candidate is consulted (persisted config.cwd fallback)') and 04-tests.md chosen-contract #1 records it, contradicting the frozen spec (CONV-018 class).
fix: Gate candidate AVAILABILITY at the caller: in DecisionQueuePanel build the candidate list as [live cwd] when known, else [config.cwd] when present, else [gitStatus.root] — one signal per REQ-009's when/ONLY-when conditions. This complies with the spec while keeping frozen TEST-324 green (it drives the pure seam directly with fixture arrays). Then, via the sanctioned test-amendment path, re-cut TEST-324's line-138 vector as an availability case and add a discriminating vector: known-but-nonmatching live cwd + member-matching config.cwd => null (fallback affordance shows). propose CONV: when a spec pins an availability-conditioned signal-preference chain ('use B only when A is unknown/absent'), the suite MUST include a discriminating vector where A is present but non-matching, so availability gating cannot silently degrade into match-failure fall-through.
resolution: Fixed per the ESC-001 re-pinned contracts: selectPaneCandidates (src/shared/decision-queue.ts:220-227) gates candidate AVAILABILITY exactly per REQ-009 ([liveCwd] when known even if non-matching; [configCwd] only when no live cwd; [gitRoot] only when neither; [] when none), matchPaneRootFromCandidates (193-202) makes the first VALID candidate DECISIVE (match or null — no match-failure fall-through; only null/undefined/'' skipped), and DecisionQueuePanel routes through the seam (lines 64-70). Pinned by amended TEST-324 (decisive-null vector), TEST-368/369 (availability set + cd'd-out discriminating vector) and structural TEST-370 — all green, freeze hashes intact.

claim: REQ-013 MUST violation: 'an entry or feature missing/mistyping the consumed fields contributes no items and throws nowhere' and 'a malformed push MUST NOT throw, MUST NOT crash the renderer'. The Verified contract defines the consumed feature fields as feature/needsHuman/reason/phase/gateN/gateM/openBlocking/lastActivityAt/detail, but buildDecisionQueue validates only needsHuman === true and feature being a non-empty string, then carries the raw object verbatim; the panel interpolates status.reason, status.phase, status.gateN/gateM and status.detail directly as React children. A malformed push whose feature mistypes any display field as an object (e.g. reason: {}) therefore BOTH contributes an item (violating 'contributes no items') AND crashes the whole renderer when the open drawer renders it — React throws 'Objects are not valid as a React child' and App.tsx has no error boundary. Today's main-side emitter sanitizes these fields, but REQ-013 deliberately treats the wire payload as untrusted; the defense the spec mandates is absent. Test adequacy: frozen TEST-312 exercises only entry-level garbage plus a missing needsHuman — a shallow pin of the per-feature clause — so the violating implementation runs green.
fix: Validate the full consumed-field set in buildDecisionQueue and skip features that mistype it (feature: non-empty string; needsHuman === true; reason: string|null; phase: string|null; gateN/gateM/openBlocking/lastActivityAt: number; detail: string), per REQ-013's 'contributes no items'; add review-loopback tests with display-field-mistype vectors (object reason/detail/phase) asserting zero items and no render throw. propose CONV: a renderer component MUST NOT interpolate wire-carried payload fields directly as React children unless the ingestion boundary validated or coerced them to primitives — one object-typed field in a malformed payload is otherwise a whole-tree render crash.
resolution: Fixed per the ESC-001 re-pinned contracts: buildDecisionQueue admits a feature only after full consumed-field validation (hasConsumableFields, src/shared/decision-queue.ts:45-55 — reason ∈ {escalation,stalled,human-review,null}; phase string|null; gateN/gateM/openBlocking/lastActivityAt Number.isFinite; detail string or absent), so object-typed fields never reach a React child and no NaN route into compareOrkyFeatures survives (unknown reason strings rejected too); the group sort is additionally NaN-hardened to the deterministic root-codepoint tie-break. Pinned by TEST-373/374 — green, freeze intact. Noted latitude: absent detail is admitted and renders empty (safe; deliberately unpinned by the loopback).

location: src/renderer/components/DecisionQueuePanel.tsx:117-150 (row onKeyDown vs nested decision-queue-open-terminal button) — REQ-014
claim: REQ-014 MUST violation: 'queue items and the fallback affordance MUST be reachable by Tab and activatable by Enter/Space'. The fallback button is nested INSIDE the role=button item row, and the row's onKeyDown catches the bubbled Enter/Space from the focused button, calls e.preventDefault() — cancelling the button's native activation (Enter's default-action click; Space's keyup click) — and runs focusProject instead, which is a guaranteed no-op for a pane-less project (no matching pane, early return). Keyboard activation of 'open terminal here' is therefore dead: Tab reaches it, Enter/Space do nothing; only the mouse path works (its onClick uses stopPropagation, but keydown propagation is unguarded). No frozen test can catch it: TEST-348/349 are source scans and e2e TEST-367 activates the fallback with a mouse click only, so REQ-014's keyboard-activation acceptance for the fallback is covered by no test. The nested native button inside a role=button ancestor is also an ARIA nested-interactive violation — the structural root of the bug.
fix: Gate the row's key handler on its own element (if (e.target !== e.currentTarget) return) or stop keydown propagation at the fallback button; better, restructure so the affordance is not a descendant of the interactive row (a trailing sibling in the row flexbox, which REQ-015's forward-compat layout already anticipates for F8 action buttons). Add a keyboard-activation assertion for the fallback in the review loopback (e2e: focus the button, press Enter, expect the terminal spawn). propose CONV: a composite clickable row that nests its own interactive children MUST gate its key/click activation handlers on e.target === e.currentTarget (or stop propagation at the child), so nested controls remain independently keyboard-activatable.
resolution: Duplicate of FINDING-008 (ux lens filed first, HIGH+contract already carried) — concurrent fan-out filing; merged per the devils-advocate lens's own recommendation.

claim: New defect introduced by the FINDING-010 fix: the drawer's unmount cleanup restores focus to the mount-time activeElement UNCONDITIONALLY, yanking focus from whatever the user focused while the non-modal drawer stayed open — most sharply from the very pane the drawer's own click-to-focus (REQ-009) just focused. Primary flow: focused in pane A -> open drawer (prev = A's xterm textarea) -> click a queue item -> setFocusedPane(B) + requestPaneFocus land DOM focus in pane B's textarea (the drawer stays open) -> close the drawer (chord, toggle, or close button) -> cleanup refocuses prev = pane A. The store's focusedPaneId remains B (setFocusedPane is wired to mousedown capture; a programmatic textarea.focus() re-stamps nothing), so the visual focus indicator says pane B while keystrokes go to pane A's shell — wrong-shell input in a terminal app, silently undoing the feature's headline gesture. The 'Modal refocus-on-close precedent' the code comment cites is only correct for a MODAL surface, where focus cannot legitimately leave while open; this drawer is non-modal and exists to move focus elsewhere. No test pins close-time focus, so the yank ships green.
fix: Make the restore conditional on the drawer still owning focus at close: at passive-cleanup time the panel DOM is already detached, so restore prev/toggle only when document.activeElement is null or document.body (focus collapsed out of the removed drawer) and otherwise leave focus where the user or focusProject put it; alternatively track focus-within via a focusout listener on the panel. Pin with a test: open the drawer from pane A, click a queue item to focus pane B, close the drawer, assert pane B's textarea keeps focus and focusedPaneId agrees. propose CONV: a NON-modal drawer/panel that restores focus on close MUST restore only when focus is still inside it at close time — never yank focus from a surface the user focused while the drawer stayed open.
resolution: Fixed exactly per the ux lens's specified remedy (coordinator-applied, one-condition change): restore-on-close now fires only when focus collapsed out of the removed drawer (activeElement is body/null); an intentional focus target (the pane the user clicked to focus) is never yanked. Residual noted: no pinning test yet — recorded for F8 (which extends these rows with actions) to pin alongside its own focus tests. Implement gate re-derived PASS after the change.
